chunk_summary: This section discusses therapeutic food availability, stock-out adaptations, and standardized outpatient CMAM visit workflows for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in India, emphasizing protocol reliability and structured care processes.
learning_objectives: Understand the variability in therapeutic food availability for SAM management in India.
Learn the importance of predefined adaptation rules during therapeutic food stock-outs.
Familiarize with the standardized outpatient CMAM visit workflow and key clinical checks.
Recognize the role of WHO 2023 guidelines in dose-reduction protocols for SAM recovery.
key_facts: India lacks a universally adopted therapeutic food product for SAM; states use RUTF, local equivalents, or state-designed supplements.
Inpatient care relies on locally prepared F-75 and F-100 recipes per MoHFW guidance.
WHO 2023 framework allows dose-reduction only after a child transitions out of SAM criteria and has no nutritional oedema.
Outpatient CMAM visits must include danger sign screening, weight measurement, and MUAC/WHZ tracking.
Adaptation rules for stock-outs should be predefined to ensure safety and consistency.
reference_quote: National technical reviews note that community-based management depends on access to a therapeutic food, yet India has not had a single universally adopted product for SAM across states.
document_key_topics: Stock-out adaptation rules for SAM management
